expectations.text: States newer/less advanced lifters generally need more hypertrophy work, not less
expectations.evidence: Beginner section: 'Hypertrophy allocation: 60–70% of training time' with clear rationale. Intermediate: 50-60%. Advanced: 40-50%. Clear progression showing beginners need most hypertrophy.

expectations.text: Describes a progression: learn lifts first, then build muscle phase, then shift toward more specific strength work as advancement increases
expectations.evidence: Beginner: 'Focus: compound movement proficiency, building a base.' Intermediate: 'Shift toward more specific strength work.' Advanced: 'Heavy, specific strength work dominates.' Clear progression.

expectations.text: Notes that high-rep bodybuilding-style work should be reduced close to competition (roughly last ~4 weeks)
expectations.evidence: In-Season section (4-8 weeks before competition): 'Hypertrophy allocation: 30–45%' with 'Accessory work becomes minimal and strategic.' Peaking block (weeks 13-16): 'Hypertrophy allocation: ~35%.' Consistent with reducing high-rep work near competition.

expectations.text: Mentions adherence/enjoyment as an important factor — a slightly suboptimal but sustainable approach still works well
expectations.evidence: Final Note: 'The best program is the one you'll execute consistently with good form. Periodization is a framework, not a law.' Captures the adherence principle.
